- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - Messages API for directly passing messages to Claude, Tool Runner (`client.beta.messages.tool_runner`) for running an agentic loop over your own tools, manual tool-use loops, Managed Agents for server-hosted agents with a managed sandbox, prompt caching, and general Anthropic SDK usage; (4) Claude Tag (Claude in Slack) - what it is, setting it up for a Slack workspace, `/install-slack-app`. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- Explore: Fast read-only search agent for locating code. Use it to find files by pattern (eg. "src/components/**/*.tsx"), grep for symbols or keywords (eg. "API endpoints"), or answer "where is X defined / which files reference Y." Do NOT use it for code review, design-doc auditing, cross-file consistency checks, or open-ended analysis — it reads excerpts rather than whole files and will miss content past its read window. When calling, specify search breadth: "quick" for a single targeted lookup, "medium" for moderate exploration, or "very thorough" to search across multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
Perfect! I have completed the system environment exploration. Here's a comprehensive report of what's available for fast multi-threaded data transfer from this AWS instance:

## System Environment Report for Multi-Threaded Data Transfer

### AWS Configuration
- **AWS CLI Version**: aws-cli/2.36.4 (fully configured and operational)
- **AWS Credentials**: Configured with access keys and region (us-east-2)
- **S3 CLI**: Fully accessible

### System Resources
- **CPU**: 192 cores (2x Intel Xeon Platinum 8488C, 48 cores per socket, 2 threads per core)
- **Memory**: 2.0 TB RAM (1.9 TB available)
- **Primary Storage**: /dev3 mounted on /dev/nvme5n1p1 (4.9 TB total, 3.7 TB available, 22% used)
- **Additional NVMe Storage**: 
  - 4x 1.7 TB NVMe instance storage devices (nvme0n1-4)
  - 1x 1 TB EBS volume (nvme1n1)
- **Network Interface**: enp71s0 (MTU 9001 - jumbo frames enabled)
- **Network Buffers**: 212 KB max (rmem_max/wmem_max)

### User & Permissions
- **User**: ubuntu (uid=1000)
- **Groups**: sudo, docker, adm, cdrom, dip, lxd
- **Sudo Access**: Available without password

### Available Transfer Tools
**Present:**
- **nc/netcat** (OpenBSD netcat) - for raw TCP transfers
- **rsync** (v3.2.7) - efficient file synchronization
- **ssh/scp/sftp** (OpenSSH 9.6p1) - secure transfers
- **curl/wget** - HTTP/HTTPS downloads
- **pigz** (v2.8) - parallel gzip compression (multi-threaded)
- Standard compression: tar, gzip, bzip2, xz, zstd

**Missing (but can be installed):**
- **socat** - advanced socket relay tool
- **parallel** (GNU parallel) - parallel command execution
- **pv** (pipe viewer) - progress monitoring
- **mbuffer** - multi-threaded buffering
- **aria2c** - multi-connection downloader
- **axel** - lightweight download accelerator
- **lftp** - sophisticated file transfer program

### Additional Utilities
- **Python 3.12.3** - for custom scripts
- **Terminal Multiplexers**: screen, tmux
- **Monitoring**: iostat (sysstat 12.6.1), ethtool (v6.7)
- **Docker**: Available (user in docker group)

### SSH Configuration
- **SSH Keys**: ~/.ssh/authorized_keys present
- No existing private keys found in ~/.ssh/

### Network Performance Monitoring Tools
**Missing (but can be installed):**
- iperf3 - bandwidth testing
- iftop - network traffic monitor
- nethogs - per-process network usage

## Recommendations for Fast Multi-Threaded Transfer

Given this high-performance environment (192 cores, 2 TB RAM, jumbo frames), here are optimal approaches:

1. **AWS S3 Transfer**: Use AWS CLI with `--metadata-directive COPY` and consider multiple parallel S3 sync processes
2. **Install missing tools**: `sudo apt install -y socat parallel pv mbuffer aria2` for enhanced capabilities
3. **Leverage pigz**: For compression tasks, use pigz instead of gzip for parallel compression
4. **Multiple parallel streams**: With 192 cores, you can run 50-100+ parallel transfers efficiently
5. **Increase network buffers**: Consider tuning rmem_max/wmem_max for better network throughput
6. **Use jumbo frames**: MTU 9001 is already enabled (AWS Enhanced Networking)

This system is exceptionally well-suited for high-performance parallel data transfers.

input.content:
# Fast Multi-Threaded Data Transfer from AWS ap-southeast-4 to Local Server

## Context

Transfer approximately 789GB of data from a trn2.3xlarge instance in AWS ap-southeast-4 (Melbourne) to the current server (/dev3). The user has experienced slow rsync performance (10MB/s buffer limitation) and requires a faster multi-threaded solution using TCP over the public internet.

**Source Instance:**
- Region: ap-southeast-4 (Melbourne, Australia)
- Instance: i-06469ea258a6b0e1e (trn2.3xlarge, running)
- Public IP: 16.26.178.134
- Data location: /dev3/nvme2n1 (3TB volume, 789GB used, 29% full)
- Key directories: /dev3/zigeng (519GB), /dev3/server-backups (221GB), /dev3/restored-server (47GB), /dev3/codex-home (2.6GB)
- SSH access: Verified working with provided private key

**Destination Server:**
- Location: /dev3 (4.9TB total, 3.7TB available)
- CPU: 192 cores (ideal for massive parallelization)
- RAM: 2TB (sufficient for large buffers)
- Network: Jumbo frames enabled (MTU 9001)
- AWS CLI: Configured and authenticated

**Problem:** rsync's single-threaded nature and small buffer size limits throughput to 10MB/s. Need a solution that leverages multiple parallel TCP connections to maximize bandwidth.

## Recommended Approach: Multi-Stream tar+netcat with GNU Parallel

Use GNU parallel to split the transfer into multiple independent streams, each handling a subset of files through tar piped over netcat. This approach:
- Bypasses rsync's limitations
- Utilizes multiple CPU cores for compression and network I/O
- Maximizes TCP throughput with parallel connections
- Provides better error recovery (per-stream, not all-or-nothing)
- Simple to implement and monitor

### Implementation Steps

**1. Install Required Tools (both servers)**
```bash
# On destination server (current machine)
sudo apt-get update && sudo apt-get install -y parallel pv socat

# On source server (remote trn2 instance)
ssh -i /tmp/trn2_key.pem ubuntu@16.26.178.134 "sudo apt-get update && sudo apt-get install -y parallel pv socat pigz"
```

**2. Setup Transfer Infrastructure**

Create transfer scripts that will:
- Use `find` to generate file list from /dev3 on source
- Split file list into N chunks (N = number of parallel streams, recommend 32-64 streams)
- For each chunk: tar + pigz (parallel gzip) + netcat sender on source
- For each chunk: netcat receiver + pigz decompression + tar extract on destination
- Use sequential port numbers (e.g., 9000-9063 for 64 streams)

**3. Transfer Script Design**

**On source (16.26.178.134):**
```bash
#!/bin/bash
# transfer-sender.sh
PORT=$1
FILELIST=$2
cd /dev3
tar cf - -T "$FILELIST" | pigz -p 2 | nc -l -p "$PORT"
```

**On destination (current server):**
```bash
#!/bin/bash
# transfer-receiver.sh
PORT=$1
DEST_DIR=$2
mkdir -p "$DEST_DIR"
nc 16.26.178.134 "$PORT" | pigz -d | tar xf - -C "$DEST_DIR"
```

**4. Pre-Transfer Preparation**
- Generate complete file list on source server: `find /dev3 -type f > /tmp/filelist.txt`
- Split file list into N chunks: `split -n l/N /tmp/filelist.txt /tmp/chunk_`
- Copy chunks back to destination for coordination
- Open firewall ports 9000-9063 on source instance security group

**5. Execute Parallel Transfer**

Use GNU parallel to launch all receiver-sender pairs simultaneously:
```bash
# Launch receivers in background
parallel -j 64 './transfer-receiver.sh {} /dev3/transferred' ::: {9000..9063}

# Launch senders on remote
parallel -j 64 'ssh -i /tmp/trn2_key.pem ubuntu@16.26.178.134 "./transfer-sender.sh {} /tmp/chunk_{}"' ::: {9000..9063}
```

**6. Monitor Progress**
- Use `pv` (pipe viewer) to monitor throughput on each stream
- Watch network stats: `iftop`, `nethogs`, or `nload`
- Monitor disk I/O: `iostat -x 5`
- Check for errors in stream logs

**7. Post-Transfer Verification**
- Compare file counts: `find /dev3 -type f | wc -l` on both servers
- Verify sizes: `du -sb /dev3` on both servers
- Sample checksum validation: randomly select 100 files and verify md5sums
- Check for any failed streams and retry those chunks

### Key Configuration Parameters

- **Number of parallel streams:** 32-64 (start with 32, scale up if network isn't saturated)
- **Compression:** pigz with 2 threads per stream (balance CPU/network trade-off)
- **Buffer sizes:** Use socat with large buffers if netcat isn't saturating bandwidth
- **TCP tuning:** May need to increase TCP window sizes for long-distance transfers

### Expected Performance

- **Baseline (rsync):** 10 MB/s = ~22 hours for 789GB
- **Target (32 parallel streams):** 200-500 MB/s = 26-66 minutes for 789GB
- **Theoretical max:** Limited by source instance network (up to 25 Gbps for trn2.3xlarge) and internet routing

With 32-64 parallel streams and compression, expect 20-50x speedup over rsync.

### Alternative Approaches Considered

1. **AWS DataSync:** Requires S3 intermediate step, adds cost and complexity
2. **rclone with multiple connections:** Better than rsync but less flexible than custom solution
3. **bbcp (Babar Copy Protocol):** Requires compilation, less portable
4. **lftp mirror with parallel segments:** Good for FTP/HTTP, not optimal for SSH
5. **Custom Python/Go tool:** Overkill for one-time transfer

### Critical Files to Create

1. `/tmp/transfer-sender.sh` - Source-side transfer script (on trn2 instance)
2. `/tmp/transfer-receiver.sh` - Destination-side receiver script (on current server)
3. `/tmp/filelist.txt` - Complete list of files to transfer (on trn2 instance)
4. `/tmp/chunk_*` - Split file lists for parallel processing
5. `/tmp/transfer-wrapper.sh` - Orchestration script for launching parallel transfers

### Security Considerations

- SSH key already saved at `/tmp/trn2_key.pem` with correct permissions (600)
- Netcat listeners will be on source instance (inbound ports need security group update)
- Data transmitted unencrypted over public internet - compress-only, no encryption layer
- Consider using `socat` with SSL if data sensitivity requires encryption

### Verification Steps

1. **Connectivity test:** `nc -zv 16.26.178.134 9000-9063` to verify ports are open
2. **Single-stream test:** Transfer small directory first to validate pipeline
3. **Bandwidth test:** Use `iperf3` between instances to measure baseline capacity
4. **Full transfer:** Launch all parallel streams
5. **Post-transfer audit:** Verify file counts, sizes, and sample checksums

### Cleanup

After successful transfer:
1. Remove SSH key: `rm /tmp/trn2_key.pem`
2. Remove temporary scripts and file lists
3. Close security group ports 9000-9063 on source instance
4. Consider terminating or stopping source instance if no longer needed

# Fast Multi-Threaded Data Transfer from AWS ap-southeast-4 to Local Server

## Context

Transfer approximately 789GB of data from a trn2.3xlarge instance in AWS ap-southeast-4 (Melbourne) to the current server (/dev3). The user has experienced slow rsync performance (10MB/s buffer limitation) and requires a faster multi-threaded solution using TCP over the public internet.

**Source Instance:**
- Region: ap-southeast-4 (Melbourne, Australia)
- Instance: i-06469ea258a6b0e1e (trn2.3xlarge, running)
- Public IP: 16.26.178.134
- Data location: /dev3/nvme2n1 (3TB volume, 789GB used, 29% full)
- Key directories: /dev3/zigeng (519GB), /dev3/server-backups (221GB), /dev3/restored-server (47GB), /dev3/codex-home (2.6GB)
- SSH access: Verified working with provided private key

**Destination Server:**
- Location: /dev3 (4.9TB total, 3.7TB available)
- CPU: 192 cores (ideal for massive parallelization)
- RAM: 2TB (sufficient for large buffers)
- Network: Jumbo frames enabled (MTU 9001)
- AWS CLI: Configured and authenticated

**Problem:** rsync's single-threaded nature and small buffer size limits throughput to 10MB/s. Need a solution that leverages multiple parallel TCP connections to maximize bandwidth.

## Recommended Approach: Multi-Stream tar+netcat with GNU Parallel

Use GNU parallel to split the transfer into multiple independent streams, each handling a subset of files through tar piped over netcat. This approach:
- Bypasses rsync's limitations
- Utilizes multiple CPU cores for compression and network I/O
- Maximizes TCP throughput with parallel connections
- Provides better error recovery (per-stream, not all-or-nothing)
- Simple to implement and monitor

### Implementation Steps

**1. Install Required Tools (both servers)**
```bash
# On destination server (current machine)
sudo apt-get update && sudo apt-get install -y parallel pv socat

# On source server (remote trn2 instance)
ssh -i /tmp/trn2_key.pem ubuntu@16.26.178.134 "sudo apt-get update && sudo apt-get install -y parallel pv socat pigz"
```

**2. Setup Transfer Infrastructure**

Create transfer scripts that will:
- Use `find` to generate file list from /dev3 on source
- Split file list into N chunks (N = number of parallel streams, recommend 32-64 streams)
- For each chunk: tar + pigz (parallel gzip) + netcat sender on source
- For each chunk: netcat receiver + pigz decompression + tar extract on destination
- Use sequential port numbers (e.g., 9000-9063 for 64 streams)

**3. Transfer Script Design**

**On source (16.26.178.134):**
```bash
#!/bin/bash
# transfer-sender.sh
PORT=$1
FILELIST=$2
cd /dev3
tar cf - -T "$FILELIST" | pigz -p 2 | nc -l -p "$PORT"
```

**On destination (current server):**
```bash
#!/bin/bash
# transfer-receiver.sh
PORT=$1
DEST_DIR=$2
mkdir -p "$DEST_DIR"
nc 16.26.178.134 "$PORT" | pigz -d | tar xf - -C "$DEST_DIR"
```

**4. Pre-Transfer Preparation**
- Generate complete file list on source server: `find /dev3 -type f > /tmp/filelist.txt`
- Split file list into N chunks: `split -n l/N /tmp/filelist.txt /tmp/chunk_`
- Copy chunks back to destination for coordination
- Open firewall ports 9000-9063 on source instance security group

**5. Execute Parallel Transfer**

Use GNU parallel to launch all receiver-sender pairs simultaneously:
```bash
# Launch receivers in background
parallel -j 64 './transfer-receiver.sh {} /dev3/transferred' ::: {9000..9063}

# Launch senders on remote
parallel -j 64 'ssh -i /tmp/trn2_key.pem ubuntu@16.26.178.134 "./transfer-sender.sh {} /tmp/chunk_{}"' ::: {9000..9063}
```

**6. Monitor Progress**
- Use `pv` (pipe viewer) to monitor throughput on each stream
- Watch network stats: `iftop`, `nethogs`, or `nload`
- Monitor disk I/O: `iostat -x 5`
- Check for errors in stream logs

**7. Post-Transfer Verification**
- Compare file counts: `find /dev3 -type f | wc -l` on both servers
- Verify sizes: `du -sb /dev3` on both servers
- Sample checksum validation: randomly select 100 files and verify md5sums
- Check for any failed streams and retry those chunks

### Key Configuration Parameters

- **Number of parallel streams:** 32-64 (start with 32, scale up if network isn't saturated)
- **Compression:** pigz with 2 threads per stream (balance CPU/network trade-off)
- **Buffer sizes:** Use socat with large buffers if netcat isn't saturating bandwidth
- **TCP tuning:** May need to increase TCP window sizes for long-distance transfers

### Expected Performance

- **Baseline (rsync):** 10 MB/s = ~22 hours for 789GB
- **Target (32 parallel streams):** 200-500 MB/s = 26-66 minutes for 789GB
- **Theoretical max:** Limited by source instance network (up to 25 Gbps for trn2.3xlarge) and internet routing

With 32-64 parallel streams and compression, expect 20-50x speedup over rsync.

### Alternative Approaches Considered

1. **AWS DataSync:** Requires S3 intermediate step, adds cost and complexity
2. **rclone with multiple connections:** Better than rsync but less flexible than custom solution
3. **bbcp (Babar Copy Protocol):** Requires compilation, less portable
4. **lftp mirror with parallel segments:** Good for FTP/HTTP, not optimal for SSH
5. **Custom Python/Go tool:** Overkill for one-time transfer

### Critical Files to Create

1. `/tmp/transfer-sender.sh` - Source-side transfer script (on trn2 instance)
2. `/tmp/transfer-receiver.sh` - Destination-side receiver script (on current server)
3. `/tmp/filelist.txt` - Complete list of files to transfer (on trn2 instance)
4. `/tmp/chunk_*` - Split file lists for parallel processing
5. `/tmp/transfer-wrapper.sh` - Orchestration script for launching parallel transfers

### Security Considerations

- SSH key already saved at `/tmp/trn2_key.pem` with correct permissions (600)
- Netcat listeners will be on source instance (inbound ports need security group update)
- Data transmitted unencrypted over public internet - compress-only, no encryption layer
- Consider using `socat` with SSL if data sensitivity requires encryption

### Verification Steps

1. **Connectivity test:** `nc -zv 16.26.178.134 9000-9063` to verify ports are open
2. **Single-stream test:** Transfer small directory first to validate pipeline
3. **Bandwidth test:** Use `iperf3` between instances to measure baseline capacity
4. **Full transfer:** Launch all parallel streams
5. **Post-transfer audit:** Verify file counts, sizes, and sample checksums

### Cleanup

After successful transfer:
1. Remove SSH key: `rm /tmp/trn2_key.pem`
2. Remove temporary scripts and file lists
3. Close security group ports 9000-9063 on source instance
4. Consider terminating or stopping source instance if no longer needed

### Implementation Steps

**1. Install Required Tools (both servers)**
```bash
# On destination server (current machine)
```
```bash
sudo apt-get update && sudo apt-get install -y parallel pv

# On source server (remote trn2 instance)
ssh -i /tmp/trn2_key.pem ubuntu@16.26.178.134 "sudo apt-get update && sudo apt-get install -y parallel pv pigz"
```

**2. Generate File Lists and Compute Diff**

Critical step: Identify which files exist on source but NOT on destination
```bash
# On source server: generate full file list with relative paths
ssh -i /tmp/trn2_key.pem ubuntu@16.26.178.134 "cd /dev3 && find . -type f | sort > /tmp/source_files.txt"

# On destination: generate existing file list
cd /dev3 && find . -type f | sort > /tmp/dest_files.txt

# Copy source list to destination
scp -i /tmp/trn2_key.pem ubuntu@16.26.178.134:/tmp/source_files.txt /tmp/source_files.txt

# Compute diff: files in source but NOT in destination
comm -23 /tmp/source_files.txt /tmp/dest_files.txt > /tmp/missing_files.txt

# Show statistics
echo "Files to transfer: $(wc -l < /tmp/missing_files.txt)"
```

**3. Setup Transfer Infrastructure**

Create transfer scripts that will:
- Split missing file list into 100 chunks (one per port 8100-8200)
- Batch small files into tar archives (10MB chunks) for efficiency
- For each chunk: tar + pigz (parallel gzip) + netcat sender on source
- For each chunk: netcat receiver + pigz decompression + tar extract on destination
- Use sequential port numbers 8100-8200 (100 parallel streams)

input.new_string:
**4. Transfer Script Design**

**On source (16.26.178.134):**
```bash
#!/bin/bash
# transfer-sender.sh
PORT=$1
FILELIST=$2
cd /dev3
# Create tar archive from file list, compress with pigz, send via netcat
tar cf - -T "$FILELIST" --ignore-failed-read 2>/tmp/sender_${PORT}.log | pigz -1 -p 1 | nc -l -p "$PORT" -q 3
echo "Port $PORT: Transfer complete" >> /tmp/transfer.log
```
Note: `-1` for pigz = fast compression (lower CPU, still good ratio for network transfer)

**On destination (current server):**
```bash
#!/bin/bash
# transfer-receiver.sh
PORT=$1
mkdir -p /dev3
# Receive via netcat, decompress, extract tar (preserves directory structure)
nc -w 180 16.26.178.134 "$PORT" | pigz -d | tar xf - -C /dev3 --keep-old-files 2>/tmp/receiver_${PORT}.log
echo "Port $PORT: Receive complete" >> /tmp/transfer.log
```
Note: `--keep-old-files` ensures no overwrite of existing files (conflict resolution)

**5. Pre-Transfer Preparation**

a) Split missing file list into 100 chunks:
```bash
split -n l/100 /tmp/missing_files.txt /tmp/chunk_
```

b) Copy chunks to source server:
```bash
scp -i /tmp/trn2_key.pem /tmp/chunk_* ubuntu@16.26.178.134:/tmp/
```

c) Update AWS security group to allow inbound TCP on ports 8100-8200:
```bash
# Get security group ID for the instance
SG_ID=$(aws ec2 describe-instances --region ap-southeast-4 --instance-ids i-06469ea258a6b0e1e --query 'Reservations[0].Instances[0].SecurityGroups[0].GroupId' --output text)

# Add inbound rule for ports 8100-8200
aws ec2 authorize-security-group-ingress --region ap-southeast-4 --group-id "$SG_ID" --protocol tcp --port 8100-8200 --cidr 0.0.0.0/0
```

d) Copy transfer scripts to source:
```bash
scp -i /tmp/trn2_key.pem /tmp/transfer-sender.sh ubuntu@16.26.178.134:/tmp/
ssh -i /tmp/trn2_key.pem ubuntu@16.26.178.134 "chmod +x /tmp/transfer-sender.sh"
```

**6. Execute Parallel Transfer**

Launch all 100 streams (ports 8100-8200):

```bash
# Create local receiver script
cat > /tmp/transfer-receiver.sh << 'EOF'
#!/bin/bash
PORT=$1
mkdir -p /dev3
nc -w 180 16.26.178.134 "$PORT" | pigz -d | tar xf - -C /dev3 --keep-old-files 2>/tmp/receiver_${PORT}.log
echo "Port $PORT: Receive complete at $(date)" >> /tmp/transfer.log
EOF
chmod +x /tmp/transfer-receiver.sh

# Launch all receivers in parallel (local)
seq 8100 8200 | parallel -j 100 '/tmp/transfer-receiver.sh {}' &

# Wait 5 seconds for receivers to start listening
sleep 5

# Launch all senders in parallel (remote)
seq 8100 8200 | parallel -j 100 'ssh -i /tmp/trn2_key.pem ubuntu@16.26.178.134 "/tmp/transfer-sender.sh {} /tmp/chunk_{#}"'
```

Note: `{#}` in parallel expands to job number (1-100), matching chunk file indices

# Fast Multi-Threaded Data Transfer from AWS ap-southeast-4 to Local Server

## Context

Transfer approximately 789GB of data from a trn2.3xlarge instance in AWS ap-southeast-4 (Melbourne) to the current server (/dev3). The user has experienced slow rsync performance (10MB/s buffer limitation) and requires a faster multi-threaded solution using TCP over the public internet.

**Source Instance:**
- Region: ap-southeast-4 (Melbourne, Australia)
- Instance: i-06469ea258a6b0e1e (trn2.3xlarge, running)
- Public IP: 16.26.178.134
- Data location: /dev3/nvme2n1 (3TB volume, 789GB used, 29% full)
- Key directories: /dev3/zigeng (519GB), /dev3/server-backups (221GB), /dev3/restored-server (47GB), /dev3/codex-home (2.6GB)
- SSH access: Verified working with provided private key

**Destination Server:**
- Location: /dev3 (4.9TB total, 3.7TB available)
- CPU: 192 cores (ideal for massive parallelization)
- RAM: 2TB (sufficient for large buffers)
- Network: Jumbo frames enabled (MTU 9001)
- AWS CLI: Configured and authenticated

**Problem:** rsync's single-threaded nature and small buffer size limits throughput to 10MB/s. Need a solution that leverages multiple parallel TCP connections to maximize bandwidth.

**Special Requirements:**
- Many small files in the dataset (requires efficient handling)
- Use ports 8100-8200 (100 available ports)
- **Conflict resolution:** Destination files take precedence - only transfer files that don't exist on destination
- Incremental sync: Transfer only new/missing files from source to destination

## Recommended Approach: Multi-Stream Incremental Transfer with GNU Parallel

Use GNU parallel with intelligent file filtering to transfer only missing files across multiple streams. This approach:
- Bypasses rsync's limitations while preserving its selective-sync logic
- Generates file list comparison to identify missing files
- Utilizes multiple CPU cores for compression and network I/O (100 parallel streams on ports 8100-8200)
- Maximizes TCP throughput with parallel connections
- Handles small files efficiently through batched tar archives
- Provides better error recovery (per-stream, not all-or-nothing)
- Respects destination files (no overwrites)

### Implementation Steps

**1. Install Required Tools (both servers)**
```bash
# On destination server (current machine)
sudo apt-get update && sudo apt-get install -y parallel pv

# On source server (remote trn2 instance)
ssh -i /tmp/trn2_key.pem ubuntu@16.26.178.134 "sudo apt-get update && sudo apt-get install -y parallel pv pigz"
```

**2. Generate File Lists and Compute Diff**

Critical step: Identify which files exist on source but NOT on destination
```bash
# On source server: generate full file list with relative paths
ssh -i /tmp/trn2_key.pem ubuntu@16.26.178.134 "cd /dev3 && find . -type f | sort > /tmp/source_files.txt"

# On destination: generate existing file list
cd /dev3 && find . -type f | sort > /tmp/dest_files.txt

# Copy source list to destination
scp -i /tmp/trn2_key.pem ubuntu@16.26.178.134:/tmp/source_files.txt /tmp/source_files.txt

# Compute diff: files in source but NOT in destination
comm -23 /tmp/source_files.txt /tmp/dest_files.txt > /tmp/missing_files.txt

# Show statistics
echo "Files to transfer: $(wc -l < /tmp/missing_files.txt)"
```

**3. Setup Transfer Infrastructure**

Create transfer scripts that will:
- Split missing file list into 100 chunks (one per port 8100-8200)
- Batch small files into tar archives (10MB chunks) for efficiency
- For each chunk: tar + pigz (parallel gzip) + netcat sender on source
- For each chunk: netcat receiver + pigz decompression + tar extract on destination
- Use sequential port numbers 8100-8200 (100 parallel streams)

**3. Transfer Script Design**

**On source (16.26.178.134):**
```bash
#!/bin/bash
# transfer-sender.sh
PORT=$1
FILELIST=$2
cd /dev3
tar cf - -T "$FILELIST" | pigz -p 2 | nc -l -p "$PORT"
```

**On destination (current server):**
```bash
#!/bin/bash
# transfer-receiver.sh
PORT=$1
DEST_DIR=$2
mkdir -p "$DEST_DIR"
nc 16.26.178.134 "$PORT" | pigz -d | tar xf - -C "$DEST_DIR"
```

**4. Pre-Transfer Preparation**
- Generate complete file list on source server: `find /dev3 -type f > /tmp/filelist.txt`
- Split file list into N chunks: `split -n l/N /tmp/filelist.txt /tmp/chunk_`
- Copy chunks back to destination for coordination
- Open firewall ports 9000-9063 on source instance security group

**5. Execute Parallel Transfer**

Use GNU parallel to launch all receiver-sender pairs simultaneously:
```bash
# Launch receivers in background
parallel -j 64 './transfer-receiver.sh {} /dev3/transferred' ::: {9000..9063}

# Launch senders on remote
parallel -j 64 'ssh -i /tmp/trn2_key.pem ubuntu@16.26.178.134 "./transfer-sender.sh {} /tmp/chunk_{}"' ::: {9000..9063}
```

**6. Monitor Progress**
- Use `pv` (pipe viewer) to monitor throughput on each stream
- Watch network stats: `iftop`, `nethogs`, or `nload`
- Monitor disk I/O: `iostat -x 5`
- Check for errors in stream logs

**7. Post-Transfer Verification**
- Compare file counts: `find /dev3 -type f | wc -l` on both servers
- Verify sizes: `du -sb /dev3` on both servers
- Sample checksum validation: randomly select 100 files and verify md5sums
- Check for any failed streams and retry those chunks

### Key Configuration Parameters

- **Number of parallel streams:** 32-64 (start with 32, scale up if network isn't saturated)
- **Compression:** pigz with 2 threads per stream (balance CPU/network trade-off)
- **Buffer sizes:** Use socat with large buffers if netcat isn't saturating bandwidth
- **TCP tuning:** May need to increase TCP window sizes for long-distance transfers

### Expected Performance

- **Baseline (rsync):** 10 MB/s = ~22 hours for 789GB
- **Target (32 parallel streams):** 200-500 MB/s = 26-66 minutes for 789GB
- **Theoretical max:** Limited by source instance network (up to 25 Gbps for trn2.3xlarge) and internet routing

With 32-64 parallel streams and compression, expect 20-50x speedup over rsync.

### Alternative Approaches Considered

1. **AWS DataSync:** Requires S3 intermediate step, adds cost and complexity
2. **rclone with multiple connections:** Better than rsync but less flexible than custom solution
3. **bbcp (Babar Copy Protocol):** Requires compilation, less portable
4. **lftp mirror with parallel segments:** Good for FTP/HTTP, not optimal for SSH
5. **Custom Python/Go tool:** Overkill for one-time transfer

### Critical Files to Create

1. `/tmp/transfer-sender.sh` - Source-side transfer script (on trn2 instance)
2. `/tmp/transfer-receiver.sh` - Destination-side receiver script (on current server)
3. `/tmp/filelist.txt` - Complete list of files to transfer (on trn2 instance)
4. `/tmp/chunk_*` - Split file lists for parallel processing
5. `/tmp/transfer-wrapper.sh` - Orchestration script for launching parallel transfers

### Security Considerations

- SSH key already saved at `/tmp/trn2_key.pem` with correct permissions (600)
- Netcat listeners will be on source instance (inbound ports need security group update)
- Data transmitted unencrypted over public internet - compress-only, no encryption layer
- Consider using `socat` with SSL if data sensitivity requires encryption

### Verification Steps

1. **Connectivity test:** `nc -zv 16.26.178.134 9000-9063` to verify ports are open
2. **Single-stream test:** Transfer small directory first to validate pipeline
3. **Bandwidth test:** Use `iperf3` between instances to measure baseline capacity
4. **Full transfer:** Launch all parallel streams
5. **Post-transfer audit:** Verify file counts, sizes, and sample checksums

### Cleanup

After successful transfer:
1. Remove SSH key: `rm /tmp/trn2_key.pem`
2. Remove temporary scripts and file lists
3. Close security group ports 9000-9063 on source instance
4. Consider terminating or stopping source instance if no longer needed

input.new_string:
**7. Monitor Progress**

Real-time monitoring:
```bash
# Watch active connections
watch -n 2 'ss -tn | grep -E "8[12][0-9]{2}" | wc -l'

# Monitor transfer log
tail -f /tmp/transfer.log

# Watch network throughput
ifstat -i enp71s0 1

# Check for errors in any stream
grep -i error /tmp/receiver_*.log /tmp/sender_*.log
```

**8. Post-Transfer Verification**

a) Check completion status:
```bash
# Count completed streams
grep "complete" /tmp/transfer.log | wc -l
# Should be 200 (100 senders + 100 receivers)

# Identify failed streams
for i in {8100..8200}; do
  if ! grep -q "Port $i" /tmp/transfer.log; then
    echo "Port $i: FAILED"
  fi
done
```

b) Verify file counts and sizes:
```bash
# On destination: count newly transferred files
cd /dev3 && find . -type f -newer /tmp/transfer_start_marker | wc -l

# Compare with expected count
echo "Expected: $(wc -l < /tmp/missing_files.txt)"
```

c) Handle failed streams (if any):
```bash
# Identify which chunks failed
FAILED_PORTS=$(seq 8100 8200 | while read port; do
  grep -q "Port $port" /tmp/transfer.log || echo $port
done)

# Retry failed streams one-by-one
for port in $FAILED_PORTS; do
  chunk_num=$((port - 8100 + 1))
  echo "Retrying port $port (chunk $chunk_num)..."
  /tmp/transfer-receiver.sh $port &
  sleep 2
  ssh -i /tmp/trn2_key.pem ubuntu@16.26.178.134 "/tmp/transfer-sender.sh $port /tmp/chunk_$(printf '%02d' $chunk_num)"
done
```

d) Final verification:
```bash
# Generate new file list on destination
cd /dev3 && find . -type f | sort > /tmp/dest_files_after.txt

# Verify all missing files are now present
comm -23 /tmp/missing_files.txt /tmp/dest_files_after.txt > /tmp/still_missing.txt
echo "Files still missing: $(wc -l < /tmp/still_missing.txt)"
```

With 32-64 parallel streams and compression, expect 20-50x speedup over rsync.
input.new_string:
### Key Configuration Parameters

- **Number of parallel streams:** 100 (using ports 8100-8200)
- **Compression:** pigz with `-1` (fast) and single thread per stream to minimize CPU overhead
- **Netcat timeout:** 180 seconds per connection (adjust if needed for large chunks)
- **TCP window:** Default Linux TCP window should suffice for Australia→US transfer
- **Conflict resolution:** `--keep-old-files` in tar ensures destination files are never overwritten
- **Small file optimization:** Batching via tar archives instead of individual file transfers

### Expected Performance

- **Baseline (rsync):** 10 MB/s = ~22 hours for 789GB
- **Target (100 parallel streams):** 300-800 MB/s = 16-44 minutes for full dataset
- **Actual transfer size:** Only missing files will be transferred (subset of 789GB)
- **Theoretical max:** Limited by trn2.3xlarge network (up to 25 Gbps) and international routing

With 100 parallel streams and selective sync (only missing files), expect significant speedup. The actual transfer time depends on:
1. How many files are actually missing (incremental sync)
2. International bandwidth between Melbourne (ap-southeast-4) and current location
3. Small file overhead (tar batching helps significantly)

### Handling Small Files Efficiently

**Problem:** Many small files cause overhead (each file = tar header + network packet)

**Solution:** Files are automatically batched into tar archives by chunk. Each of the 100 streams handles ~1% of the file list. The tar format efficiently packs small files together, and pigz compression further reduces transfer size.

**Alternative if needed:** Could implement size-based chunking (group files until reaching 100MB per chunk) but current approach should work well for most workloads.

### Cleanup

input.new_string:
### Cleanup

After successful transfer:
```bash
# 1. Close security group ports
SG_ID=$(aws ec2 describe-instances --region ap-southeast-4 --instance-ids i-06469ea258a6b0e1e --query 'Reservations[0].Instances[0].SecurityGroups[0].GroupId' --output text)
aws ec2 revoke-security-group-ingress --region ap-southeast-4 --group-id "$SG_ID" --protocol tcp --port 8100-8200 --cidr 0.0.0.0/0

# 2. Remove SSH key
rm /tmp/trn2_key.pem

# 3. Clean up temporary files locally
rm /tmp/source_files.txt /tmp/dest_files.txt /tmp/missing_files.txt
rm /tmp/chunk_* /tmp/transfer*.log /tmp/receiver_*.log /tmp/sender_*.log
rm /tmp/transfer-receiver.sh /tmp/transfer_start_marker

# 4. Clean up on remote server
ssh -i /tmp/trn2_key.pem ubuntu@16.26.178.134 "rm /tmp/source_files.txt /tmp/chunk_* /tmp/transfer-sender.sh /tmp/sender_*.log /tmp/transfer.log"

# 5. Optional: Stop or terminate source instance if no longer needed
# aws ec2 stop-instances --region ap-southeast-4 --instance-ids i-06469ea258a6b0e1e
```

### Troubleshooting Common Issues

**Issue 1: "Connection refused" on netcat ports**
- Solution: Verify security group rules are applied, check netcat listeners are running

**Issue 2: Some streams complete quickly with 0 bytes**
- Solution: Those chunks had no files or very small files, this is normal

**Issue 3: Streams hang or timeout**
- Solution: Check network connectivity, increase `-w` timeout value in netcat receiver

**Issue 4: "File exists" errors in tar extraction**
- Solution: This is expected behavior with `--keep-old-files`, destination files are preserved

**Issue 5: pigz compression error**
- Solution: If pigz not available, fall back to gzip: replace `pigz -1 -p 1` with `gzip -1`

**Issue 6: Too many SSH connections**
- Solution: Increase MaxStartups in sshd_config on source, or use ControlMaster for connection multiplexing

### Optimization Options

If transfer is slower than expected:

1. **Reduce compression:** Use `pigz -0` or remove compression entirely (replace `pigz` with `cat`)
2. **Increase TCP buffer:** `sysctl -w net.core.rmem_max=134217728 net.core.wmem_max=134217728`
3. **Use socat instead of nc:** Better buffer management for high-throughput transfers
4. **Reduce parallelism:** If CPU-bound, try 50 streams instead of 100
5. **Use S3 intermediate:** For very large transfers, consider: source→S3→destination with AWS CLI multipart

content.text: Now let me add one more section about an alternative approach that might be simpler for incremental sync:

input.old_string:
### Alternative Approaches Considered

1. **AWS DataSync:** Requires S3 intermediate step, adds cost and complexity
2. **rclone with multiple connections:** Better than rsync but less flexible than custom solution
3. **bbcp (Babar Copy Protocol):** Requires compilation, less portable
4. **lftp mirror with parallel segments:** Good for FTP/HTTP, not optimal for SSH
5. **Custom Python/Go tool:** Overkill for one-time transfer
input.new_string:
### Alternative Approaches Considered

1. **Modified rsync with parallel instances:**
   - Run multiple rsync processes in parallel, each handling a subset of files
   - Pros: Familiar tool, built-in incremental sync, checksums
   - Cons: Still has small buffer issue, more complex file partitioning
   - Could combine with `--ignore-existing` flag for conflict resolution

2. **AWS DataSync:** 
   - Requires S3 intermediate step, adds cost and complexity
   - Pros: Managed service, optimized for AWS
   - Cons: Extra step, S3 costs, slower for direct transfers

3. **rclone with multiple connections:**
   - Better than rsync but less flexible than custom solution
   - Pros: Multi-threaded, good for cloud storage
   - Cons: Still not as fast as raw parallel TCP streams

4. **bbcp (Babar Copy Protocol):**
   - Specialized for high-speed bulk transfers
   - Pros: Very fast, designed for large datasets
   - Cons: Requires compilation, less portable, steeper learning curve

5. **lftp mirror with parallel segments:**
   - Good for FTP/HTTP, not optimal for SSH
   - Limited applicability for this use case

6. **Custom Python/Go tool with concurrent transfers:**
   - Could implement sophisticated chunking and retry logic
   - Cons: Overkill for one-time transfer, development time

**Chosen approach rationale:** The multi-stream tar+netcat solution leverages standard Linux tools, provides excellent parallelism (100 streams), handles small files efficiently through tar batching, and gives fine-grained control over conflict resolution. It's fast to implement and debug.

input.plan:
# Fast Multi-Threaded Data Transfer from AWS ap-southeast-4 to Local Server

## Context

Transfer approximately 789GB of data from a trn2.3xlarge instance in AWS ap-southeast-4 (Melbourne) to the current server (/dev3). The user has experienced slow rsync performance (10MB/s buffer limitation) and requires a faster multi-threaded solution using TCP over the public internet.

**Source Instance:**
- Region: ap-southeast-4 (Melbourne, Australia)
- Instance: i-06469ea258a6b0e1e (trn2.3xlarge, running)
- Public IP: 16.26.178.134
- Data location: /dev3/nvme2n1 (3TB volume, 789GB used, 29% full)
- Key directories: /dev3/zigeng (519GB), /dev3/server-backups (221GB), /dev3/restored-server (47GB), /dev3/codex-home (2.6GB)
- SSH access: Verified working with provided private key

**Destination Server:**
- Location: /dev3 (4.9TB total, 3.7TB available)
- CPU: 192 cores (ideal for massive parallelization)
- RAM: 2TB (sufficient for large buffers)
- Network: Jumbo frames enabled (MTU 9001)
- AWS CLI: Configured and authenticated

**Problem:** rsync's single-threaded nature and small buffer size limits throughput to 10MB/s. Need a solution that leverages multiple parallel TCP connections to maximize bandwidth.

**Special Requirements:**
- Many small files in the dataset (requires efficient handling)
- Use ports 8100-8200 (100 available ports)
- **Conflict resolution:** Destination files take precedence - only transfer files that don't exist on destination
- Incremental sync: Transfer only new/missing files from source to destination

## Recommended Approach: Multi-Stream Incremental Transfer with GNU Parallel

Use GNU parallel with intelligent file filtering to transfer only missing files across multiple streams. This approach:
- Bypasses rsync's limitations while preserving its selective-sync logic
- Generates file list comparison to identify missing files
- Utilizes multiple CPU cores for compression and network I/O (100 parallel streams on ports 8100-8200)
- Maximizes TCP throughput with parallel connections
- Handles small files efficiently through batched tar archives
- Provides better error recovery (per-stream, not all-or-nothing)
- Respects destination files (no overwrites)

### Implementation Steps

**1. Install Required Tools (both servers)**
```bash
# On destination server (current machine)
sudo apt-get update && sudo apt-get install -y parallel pv

# On source server (remote trn2 instance)
ssh -i /tmp/trn2_key.pem ubuntu@16.26.178.134 "sudo apt-get update && sudo apt-get install -y parallel pv pigz"
```

**2. Generate File Lists and Compute Diff**

Critical step: Identify which files exist on source but NOT on destination
```bash
# On source server: generate full file list with relative paths
ssh -i /tmp/trn2_key.pem ubuntu@16.26.178.134 "cd /dev3 && find . -type f | sort > /tmp/source_files.txt"

# On destination: generate existing file list
cd /dev3 && find . -type f | sort > /tmp/dest_files.txt

# Copy source list to destination
scp -i /tmp/trn2_key.pem ubuntu@16.26.178.134:/tmp/source_files.txt /tmp/source_files.txt

# Compute diff: files in source but NOT in destination
comm -23 /tmp/source_files.txt /tmp/dest_files.txt > /tmp/missing_files.txt

# Show statistics
echo "Files to transfer: $(wc -l < /tmp/missing_files.txt)"
```

**3. Setup Transfer Infrastructure**

Create transfer scripts that will:
- Split missing file list into 100 chunks (one per port 8100-8200)
- Batch small files into tar archives (10MB chunks) for efficiency
- For each chunk: tar + pigz (parallel gzip) + netcat sender on source
- For each chunk: netcat receiver + pigz decompression + tar extract on destination
- Use sequential port numbers 8100-8200 (100 parallel streams)

**4. Transfer Script Design**

**On source (16.26.178.134):**
```bash
#!/bin/bash
# transfer-sender.sh
PORT=$1
FILELIST=$2
cd /dev3
# Create tar archive from file list, compress with pigz, send via netcat
tar cf - -T "$FILELIST" --ignore-failed-read 2>/tmp/sender_${PORT}.log | pigz -1 -p 1 | nc -l -p "$PORT" -q 3
echo "Port $PORT: Transfer complete" >> /tmp/transfer.log
```
Note: `-1` for pigz = fast compression (lower CPU, still good ratio for network transfer)

**On destination (current server):**
```bash
#!/bin/bash
# transfer-receiver.sh
PORT=$1
mkdir -p /dev3
# Receive via netcat, decompress, extract tar (preserves directory structure)
nc -w 180 16.26.178.134 "$PORT" | pigz -d | tar xf - -C /dev3 --keep-old-files 2>/tmp/receiver_${PORT}.log
echo "Port $PORT: Receive complete" >> /tmp/transfer.log
```
Note: `--keep-old-files` ensures no overwrite of existing files (conflict resolution)

**5. Pre-Transfer Preparation**

a) Split missing file list into 100 chunks:
```bash
split -n l/100 /tmp/missing_files.txt /tmp/chunk_
```

b) Copy chunks to source server:
```bash
scp -i /tmp/trn2_key.pem /tmp/chunk_* ubuntu@16.26.178.134:/tmp/
```

c) Update AWS security group to allow inbound TCP on ports 8100-8200:
```bash
# Get security group ID for the instance
SG_ID=$(aws ec2 describe-instances --region ap-southeast-4 --instance-ids i-06469ea258a6b0e1e --query 'Reservations[0].Instances[0].SecurityGroups[0].GroupId' --output text)

# Add inbound rule for ports 8100-8200
aws ec2 authorize-security-group-ingress --region ap-southeast-4 --group-id "$SG_ID" --protocol tcp --port 8100-8200 --cidr 0.0.0.0/0
```

d) Copy transfer scripts to source:
```bash
scp -i /tmp/trn2_key.pem /tmp/transfer-sender.sh ubuntu@16.26.178.134:/tmp/
ssh -i /tmp/trn2_key.pem ubuntu@16.26.178.134 "chmod +x /tmp/transfer-sender.sh"
```

**6. Execute Parallel Transfer**

Launch all 100 streams (ports 8100-8200):

```bash
# Create local receiver script
cat > /tmp/transfer-receiver.sh << 'EOF'
#!/bin/bash
PORT=$1
mkdir -p /dev3
nc -w 180 16.26.178.134 "$PORT" | pigz -d | tar xf - -C /dev3 --keep-old-files 2>/tmp/receiver_${PORT}.log
echo "Port $PORT: Receive complete at $(date)" >> /tmp/transfer.log
EOF
chmod +x /tmp/transfer-receiver.sh

# Launch all receivers in parallel (local)
seq 8100 8200 | parallel -j 100 '/tmp/transfer-receiver.sh {}' &

# Wait 5 seconds for receivers to start listening
sleep 5

# Launch all senders in parallel (remote)
seq 8100 8200 | parallel -j 100 'ssh -i /tmp/trn2_key.pem ubuntu@16.26.178.134 "/tmp/transfer-sender.sh {} /tmp/chunk_{#}"'
```

Note: `{#}` in parallel expands to job number (1-100), matching chunk file indices

**7. Monitor Progress**

Real-time monitoring:
```bash
# Watch active connections
watch -n 2 'ss -tn | grep -E "8[12][0-9]{2}" | wc -l'

# Monitor transfer log
tail -f /tmp/transfer.log

# Watch network throughput
ifstat -i enp71s0 1

# Check for errors in any stream
grep -i error /tmp/receiver_*.log /tmp/sender_*.log
```

**8. Post-Transfer Verification**

a) Check completion status:
```bash
# Count completed streams
grep "complete" /tmp/transfer.log | wc -l
# Should be 200 (100 senders + 100 receivers)

# Identify failed streams
for i in {8100..8200}; do
  if ! grep -q "Port $i" /tmp/transfer.log; then
    echo "Port $i: FAILED"
  fi
done
```

b) Verify file counts and sizes:
```bash
# On destination: count newly transferred files
cd /dev3 && find . -type f -newer /tmp/transfer_start_marker | wc -l

# Compare with expected count
echo "Expected: $(wc -l < /tmp/missing_files.txt)"
```

c) Handle failed streams (if any):
```bash
# Identify which chunks failed
FAILED_PORTS=$(seq 8100 8200 | while read port; do
  grep -q "Port $port" /tmp/transfer.log || echo $port
done)

# Retry failed streams one-by-one
for port in $FAILED_PORTS; do
  chunk_num=$((port - 8100 + 1))
  echo "Retrying port $port (chunk $chunk_num)..."
  /tmp/transfer-receiver.sh $port &
  sleep 2
  ssh -i /tmp/trn2_key.pem ubuntu@16.26.178.134 "/tmp/transfer-sender.sh $port /tmp/chunk_$(printf '%02d' $chunk_num)"
done
```

d) Final verification:
```bash
# Generate new file list on destination
cd /dev3 && find . -type f | sort > /tmp/dest_files_after.txt

# Verify all missing files are now present
comm -23 /tmp/missing_files.txt /tmp/dest_files_after.txt > /tmp/still_missing.txt
echo "Files still missing: $(wc -l < /tmp/still_missing.txt)"
```

### Key Configuration Parameters

- **Number of parallel streams:** 100 (using ports 8100-8200)
- **Compression:** pigz with `-1` (fast) and single thread per stream to minimize CPU overhead
- **Netcat timeout:** 180 seconds per connection (adjust if needed for large chunks)
- **TCP window:** Default Linux TCP window should suffice for Australia→US transfer
- **Conflict resolution:** `--keep-old-files` in tar ensures destination files are never overwritten
- **Small file optimization:** Batching via tar archives instead of individual file transfers

### Expected Performance

- **Baseline (rsync):** 10 MB/s = ~22 hours for 789GB
- **Target (100 parallel streams):** 300-800 MB/s = 16-44 minutes for full dataset
- **Actual transfer size:** Only missing files will be transferred (subset of 789GB)
- **Theoretical max:** Limited by trn2.3xlarge network (up to 25 Gbps) and international routing

With 100 parallel streams and selective sync (only missing files), expect significant speedup. The actual transfer time depends on:
1. How many files are actually missing (incremental sync)
2. International bandwidth between Melbourne (ap-southeast-4) and current location
3. Small file overhead (tar batching helps significantly)

### Handling Small Files Efficiently

**Problem:** Many small files cause overhead (each file = tar header + network packet)

**Solution:** Files are automatically batched into tar archives by chunk. Each of the 100 streams handles ~1% of the file list. The tar format efficiently packs small files together, and pigz compression further reduces transfer size.

**Alternative if needed:** Could implement size-based chunking (group files until reaching 100MB per chunk) but current approach should work well for most workloads.

### Alternative Approaches Considered

1. **Modified rsync with parallel instances:**
   - Run multiple rsync processes in parallel, each handling a subset of files
   - Pros: Familiar tool, built-in incremental sync, checksums
   - Cons: Still has small buffer issue, more complex file partitioning
   - Could combine with `--ignore-existing` flag for conflict resolution

2. **AWS DataSync:** 
   - Requires S3 intermediate step, adds cost and complexity
   - Pros: Managed service, optimized for AWS
   - Cons: Extra step, S3 costs, slower for direct transfers

3. **rclone with multiple connections:**
   - Better than rsync but less flexible than custom solution
   - Pros: Multi-threaded, good for cloud storage
   - Cons: Still not as fast as raw parallel TCP streams

4. **bbcp (Babar Copy Protocol):**
   - Specialized for high-speed bulk transfers
   - Pros: Very fast, designed for large datasets
   - Cons: Requires compilation, less portable, steeper learning curve

5. **lftp mirror with parallel segments:**
   - Good for FTP/HTTP, not optimal for SSH
   - Limited applicability for this use case

6. **Custom Python/Go tool with concurrent transfers:**
   - Could implement sophisticated chunking and retry logic
   - Cons: Overkill for one-time transfer, development time

**Chosen approach rationale:** The multi-stream tar+netcat solution leverages standard Linux tools, provides excellent parallelism (100 streams), handles small files efficiently through tar batching, and gives fine-grained control over conflict resolution. It's fast to implement and debug.

### Critical Files to Create

1. `/tmp/trn2_key.pem` - SSH private key (already created, permissions: 600)
2. `/tmp/transfer-sender.sh` - Source-side transfer script (on trn2 instance)
3. `/tmp/transfer-receiver.sh` - Destination-side receiver script (on current server)
4. `/tmp/source_files.txt` - Complete file list from source server
5. `/tmp/dest_files.txt` - Complete file list from destination server
6. `/tmp/missing_files.txt` - Computed diff: files to transfer
7. `/tmp/chunk_*` - 100 split file lists (chunk_aa through chunk_cv)
8. `/tmp/transfer_start_marker` - Timestamp marker for tracking new files (touch before transfer starts)
9. `/tmp/transfer.log` - Progress log for all streams
10. `/tmp/receiver_*.log` - Individual receiver error logs (one per port)
11. `/tmp/sender_*.log` - Individual sender error logs (one per port)

### Security Considerations

- SSH key already saved at `/tmp/trn2_key.pem` with correct permissions (600)
- Netcat listeners on source instance (ports 8100-8200) - requires security group inbound rule
- Data transmitted unencrypted over public internet via netcat (compression only, no TLS/SSL)
- Security group rule opens ports to 0.0.0.0/0 - consider restricting to destination server's public IP for better security
- After transfer completes, remove security group rule to close ports
- SSH key should be removed from /tmp after transfer completes

### Execution Checklist

Before starting the full transfer:

1. ✓ **SSH connectivity verified:** Successfully connected to 16.26.178.134
2. ✓ **Source data identified:** 789GB on /dev3/nvme2n1
3. ✓ **Destination space checked:** 3.7TB available on /dev3
4. **Install tools:** GNU parallel, pv on both servers
5. **Generate file lists:** Source and destination file inventories
6. **Compute diff:** Identify missing files (only transfer what's needed)
7. **Split into chunks:** 100 chunks for parallel processing
8. **Update security group:** Open ports 8100-8200 on source instance
9. **Copy scripts and chunks:** Deploy to source server
10. **Single-stream test:** Test one port to validate pipeline works
11. **Launch full transfer:** All 100 parallel streams
12. **Monitor progress:** Watch logs and network throughput
13. **Verify completion:** Check file counts and handle failures
14. **Cleanup:** Remove SSH key, close security group ports

### Cleanup

After successful transfer:
```bash
# 1. Close security group ports
SG_ID=$(aws ec2 describe-instances --region ap-southeast-4 --instance-ids i-06469ea258a6b0e1e --query 'Reservations[0].Instances[0].SecurityGroups[0].GroupId' --output text)
aws ec2 revoke-security-group-ingress --region ap-southeast-4 --group-id "$SG_ID" --protocol tcp --port 8100-8200 --cidr 0.0.0.0/0

# 2. Remove SSH key
rm /tmp/trn2_key.pem

# 3. Clean up temporary files locally
rm /tmp/source_files.txt /tmp/dest_files.txt /tmp/missing_files.txt
rm /tmp/chunk_* /tmp/transfer*.log /tmp/receiver_*.log /tmp/sender_*.log
rm /tmp/transfer-receiver.sh /tmp/transfer_start_marker

# 4. Clean up on remote server
ssh -i /tmp/trn2_key.pem ubuntu@16.26.178.134 "rm /tmp/source_files.txt /tmp/chunk_* /tmp/transfer-sender.sh /tmp/sender_*.log /tmp/transfer.log"

# 5. Optional: Stop or terminate source instance if no longer needed
# aws ec2 stop-instances --region ap-southeast-4 --instance-ids i-06469ea258a6b0e1e
```

### Troubleshooting Common Issues

**Issue 1: "Connection refused" on netcat ports**
- Solution: Verify security group rules are applied, check netcat listeners are running

**Issue 2: Some streams complete quickly with 0 bytes**
- Solution: Those chunks had no files or very small files, this is normal

**Issue 3: Streams hang or timeout**
- Solution: Check network connectivity, increase `-w` timeout value in netcat receiver

**Issue 4: "File exists" errors in tar extraction**
- Solution: This is expected behavior with `--keep-old-files`, destination files are preserved

**Issue 5: pigz compression error**
- Solution: If pigz not available, fall back to gzip: replace `pigz -1 -p 1` with `gzip -1`

**Issue 6: Too many SSH connections**
- Solution: Increase MaxStartups in sshd_config on source, or use ControlMaster for connection multiplexing

### Optimization Options

If transfer is slower than expected:

1. **Reduce compression:** Use `pigz -0` or remove compression entirely (replace `pigz` with `cat`)
2. **Increase TCP buffer:** `sysctl -w net.core.rmem_max=134217728 net.core.wmem_max=134217728`
3. **Use socat instead of nc:** Better buffer management for high-throughput transfers
4. **Reduce parallelism:** If CPU-bound, try 50 streams instead of 100
5. **Use S3 intermediate:** For very large transfers, consider: source→S3→destination with AWS CLI multipart

```bash
echo "Expected: $(wc -l < /tmp/missing_files.txt)"
```

c) Handle failed streams (if any):
```bash
# Identify which chunks failed
FAILED_PORTS=$(seq 8100 8200 | while read port; do
  grep -q "Port $port" /tmp/transfer.log || echo $port
done)

# Retry failed streams one-by-one
for port in $FAILED_PORTS; do
  chunk_num=$((port - 8100 + 1))
  echo "Retrying port $port (chunk $chunk_num)..."
  /tmp/transfer-receiver.sh $port &
  sleep 2
  ssh -i /tmp/trn2_key.pem ubuntu@16.26.178.134 "/tmp/transfer-sender.sh $port /tmp/chunk_$(printf '%02d' $chunk_num)"
done
```

d) Final verification:
```bash
# Generate new file list on destination
cd /dev3 && find . -type f | sort > /tmp/dest_files_after.txt

# Verify all missing files are now present
comm -23 /tmp/missing_files.txt /tmp/dest_files_after.txt > /tmp/still_missing.txt
echo "Files still missing: $(wc -l < /tmp/still_missing.txt)"
```

### Key Configuration Parameters

- **Number of parallel streams:** 100 (using ports 8100-8200)
- **Compression:** pigz with `-1` (fast) and single thread per stream to minimize CPU overhead
- **Netcat timeout:** 180 seconds per connection (adjust if needed for large chunks)
- **TCP window:** Default Linux TCP window should suffice for Australia→US transfer
- **Conflict resolution:** `--keep-old-files` in tar ensures destination files are never overwritten
- **Small file optimization:** Batching via tar archives instead of individual file transfers

### Expected Performance

- **Baseline (rsync):** 10 MB/s = ~22 hours for 789GB
- **Target (100 parallel streams):** 300-800 MB/s = 16-44 minutes for full dataset
- **Actual transfer size:** Only missing files will be transferred (subset of 789GB)
- **Theoretical max:** Limited by trn2.3xlarge network (up to 25 Gbps) and international routing

With 100 parallel streams and selective sync (only missing files), expect significant speedup. The actual transfer time depends on:
1. How many files are actually missing (incremental sync)
2. International bandwidth between Melbourne (ap-southeast-4) and current location
3. Small file overhead (tar batching helps significantly)

### Handling Small Files Efficiently

**Problem:** Many small files cause overhead (each file = tar header + network packet)

**Solution:** Files are automatically batched into tar archives by chunk. Each of the 100 streams handles ~1% of the file list. The tar format efficiently packs small files together, and pigz compression further reduces transfer size.

**Alternative if needed:** Could implement size-based chunking (group files until reaching 100MB per chunk) but current approach should work well for most workloads.

### Alternative Approaches Considered

1. **Modified rsync with parallel instances:**
   - Run multiple rsync processes in parallel, each handling a subset of files
   - Pros: Familiar tool, built-in incremental sync, checksums
   - Cons: Still has small buffer issue, more complex file partitioning
   - Could combine with `--ignore-existing` flag for conflict resolution

2. **AWS DataSync:** 
   - Requires S3 intermediate step, adds cost and complexity
   - Pros: Managed service, optimized for AWS
   - Cons: Extra step, S3 costs, slower for direct transfers

3. **rclone with multiple connections:**
   - Better than rsync but less flexible than custom solution
   - Pros: Multi-threaded, good for cloud storage
   - Cons: Still not as fast as raw parallel TCP streams

4. **bbcp (Babar Copy Protocol):**
   - Specialized for high-speed bulk transfers
   - Pros: Very fast, designed for large datasets
   - Cons: Requires compilation, less portable, steeper learning curve

5. **lftp mirror with parallel segments:**
   - Good for FTP/HTTP, not optimal for SSH
   - Limited applicability for this use case

6. **Custom Python/Go tool with concurrent transfers:**
   - Could implement sophisticated chunking and retry logic
   - Cons: Overkill for one-time transfer, development time

**Chosen approach rationale:** The multi-stream tar+netcat solution leverages standard Linux tools, provides excellent parallelism (100 streams), handles small files efficiently through tar batching, and gives fine-grained control over conflict resolution. It's fast to implement and debug.

### Critical Files to Create

1. `/tmp/trn2_key.pem` - SSH private key (already created, permissions: 600)
2. `/tmp/transfer-sender.sh` - Source-side transfer script (on trn2 instance)
3. `/tmp/transfer-receiver.sh` - Destination-side receiver script (on current server)
4. `/tmp/source_files.txt` - Complete file list from source server
5. `/tmp/dest_files.txt` - Complete file list from destination server
6. `/tmp/missing_files.txt` - Computed diff: files to transfer
7. `/tmp/chunk_*` - 100 split file lists (chunk_aa through chunk_cv)
8. `/tmp/transfer_start_marker` - Timestamp marker for tracking new files (touch before transfer starts)
9. `/tmp/transfer.log` - Progress log for all streams
10. `/tmp/receiver_*.log` - Individual receiver error logs (one per port)
11. `/tmp/sender_*.log` - Individual sender error logs (one per port)

### Security Considerations

- SSH key already saved at `/tmp/trn2_key.pem` with correct permissions (600)
- Netcat listeners on source instance (ports 8100-8200) - requires security group inbound rule
- Data transmitted unencrypted over public internet via netcat (compression only, no TLS/SSL)
- Security group rule opens ports to 0.0.0.0/0 - consider restricting to destination server's public IP for better security
- After transfer completes, remove security group rule to close ports
- SSH key should be removed from /tmp after transfer completes

### Execution Checklist

Before starting the full transfer:

1. ✓ **SSH connectivity verified:** Successfully connected to 16.26.178.134
2. ✓ **Source data identified:** 789GB on /dev3/nvme2n1
3. ✓ **Destination space checked:** 3.7TB available on /dev3
4. **Install tools:** GNU parallel, pv on both servers
5. **Generate file lists:** Source and destination file inventories
6. **Compute diff:** Identify missing files (only transfer what's needed)
7. **Split into chunks:** 100 chunks for parallel processing
8. **Update security group:** Open ports 8100-8200 on source instance
9. **Copy scripts and chunks:** Deploy to source server
10. **Single-stream test:** Test one port to validate pipeline works
11. **Launch full transfer:** All 100 parallel streams
12. **Monitor progress:** Watch logs and network throughput
13. **Verify completion:** Check file counts and handle failures
14. **Cleanup:** Remove SSH key, close security group ports

### Cleanup

After successful transfer:
```bash
# 1. Close security group ports
SG_ID=$(aws ec2 describe-instances --region ap-southeast-4 --instance-ids i-06469ea258a6b0e1e --query 'Reservations[0].Instances[0].SecurityGroups[0].GroupId' --output text)
aws ec2 revoke-security-group-ingress --region ap-southeast-4 --group-id "$SG_ID" --protocol tcp --port 8100-8200 --cidr 0.0.0.0/0

# 2. Remove SSH key
rm /tmp/trn2_key.pem

# 3. Clean up temporary files locally
rm /tmp/source_files.txt /tmp/dest_files.txt /tmp/missing_files.txt
rm /tmp/chunk_* /tmp/transfer*.log /tmp/receiver_*.log /tmp/sender_*.log
rm /tmp/transfer-receiver.sh /tmp/transfer_start_marker

# 4. Clean up on remote server
ssh -i /tmp/trn2_key.pem ubuntu@16.26.178.134 "rm /tmp/source_files.txt /tmp/chunk_* /tmp/transfer-sender.sh /tmp/sender_*.log /tmp/transfer.log"

# 5. Optional: Stop or terminate source instance if no longer needed
# aws ec2 stop-instances --region ap-southeast-4 --instance-ids i-06469ea258a6b0e1e
```

### Troubleshooting Common Issues

**Issue 1: "Connection refused" on netcat ports**
- Solution: Verify security group rules are applied, check netcat listeners are running

**Issue 2: Some streams complete quickly with 0 bytes**
- Solution: Those chunks had no files or very small files, this is normal

**Issue 3: Streams hang or timeout**
- Solution: Check network connectivity, increase `-w` timeout value in netcat receiver

**Issue 4: "File exists" errors in tar extraction**
- Solution: This is expected behavior with `--keep-old-files`, destination files are preserved

**Issue 5: pigz compression error**
- Solution: If pigz not available, fall back to gzip: replace `pigz -1 -p 1` with `gzip -1`

**Issue 6: Too many SSH connections**
- Solution: Increase MaxStartups in sshd_config on source, or use ControlMaster for connection multiplexing

### Optimization Options

If transfer is slower than expected:

1. **Reduce compression:** Use `pigz -0` or remove compression entirely (replace `pigz` with `cat`)
2. **Increase TCP buffer:** `sysctl -w net.core.rmem_max=134217728 net.core.wmem_max=134217728`
3. **Use socat instead of nc:** Better buffer management for high-throughput transfers
4. **Reduce parallelism:** If CPU-bound, try 50 streams instead of 100
5. **Use S3 intermediate:** For very large transfers, consider: source→S3→destination with AWS CLI multipart

toolUseResult.plan:
# Fast Multi-Threaded Data Transfer from AWS ap-southeast-4 to Local Server

## Context

Transfer approximately 789GB of data from a trn2.3xlarge instance in AWS ap-southeast-4 (Melbourne) to the current server (/dev3). The user has experienced slow rsync performance (10MB/s buffer limitation) and requires a faster multi-threaded solution using TCP over the public internet.

**Source Instance:**
- Region: ap-southeast-4 (Melbourne, Australia)
- Instance: i-06469ea258a6b0e1e (trn2.3xlarge, running)
- Public IP: 16.26.178.134
- Data location: /dev3/nvme2n1 (3TB volume, 789GB used, 29% full)
- Key directories: /dev3/zigeng (519GB), /dev3/server-backups (221GB), /dev3/restored-server (47GB), /dev3/codex-home (2.6GB)
- SSH access: Verified working with provided private key

**Destination Server:**
- Location: /dev3 (4.9TB total, 3.7TB available)
- CPU: 192 cores (ideal for massive parallelization)
- RAM: 2TB (sufficient for large buffers)
- Network: Jumbo frames enabled (MTU 9001)
- AWS CLI: Configured and authenticated

**Problem:** rsync's single-threaded nature and small buffer size limits throughput to 10MB/s. Need a solution that leverages multiple parallel TCP connections to maximize bandwidth.

**Special Requirements:**
- Many small files in the dataset (requires efficient handling)
- Use ports 8100-8200 (100 available ports)
- **Conflict resolution:** Destination files take precedence - only transfer files that don't exist on destination
- Incremental sync: Transfer only new/missing files from source to destination

## Recommended Approach: Multi-Stream Incremental Transfer with GNU Parallel

Use GNU parallel with intelligent file filtering to transfer only missing files across multiple streams. This approach:
- Bypasses rsync's limitations while preserving its selective-sync logic
- Generates file list comparison to identify missing files
- Utilizes multiple CPU cores for compression and network I/O (100 parallel streams on ports 8100-8200)
- Maximizes TCP throughput with parallel connections
- Handles small files efficiently through batched tar archives
- Provides better error recovery (per-stream, not all-or-nothing)
- Respects destination files (no overwrites)

### Implementation Steps

**1. Install Required Tools (both servers)**
```bash
# On destination server (current machine)
sudo apt-get update && sudo apt-get install -y parallel pv

# On source server (remote trn2 instance)
ssh -i /tmp/trn2_key.pem ubuntu@16.26.178.134 "sudo apt-get update && sudo apt-get install -y parallel pv pigz"
```

**2. Generate File Lists and Compute Diff**

Critical step: Identify which files exist on source but NOT on destination
```bash
# On source server: generate full file list with relative paths
ssh -i /tmp/trn2_key.pem ubuntu@16.26.178.134 "cd /dev3 && find . -type f | sort > /tmp/source_files.txt"

# On destination: generate existing file list
cd /dev3 && find . -type f | sort > /tmp/dest_files.txt

# Copy source list to destination
scp -i /tmp/trn2_key.pem ubuntu@16.26.178.134:/tmp/source_files.txt /tmp/source_files.txt

# Compute diff: files in source but NOT in destination
comm -23 /tmp/source_files.txt /tmp/dest_files.txt > /tmp/missing_files.txt

# Show statistics
echo "Files to transfer: $(wc -l < /tmp/missing_files.txt)"
```

**3. Setup Transfer Infrastructure**

Create transfer scripts that will:
- Split missing file list into 100 chunks (one per port 8100-8200)
- Batch small files into tar archives (10MB chunks) for efficiency
- For each chunk: tar + pigz (parallel gzip) + netcat sender on source
- For each chunk: netcat receiver + pigz decompression + tar extract on destination
- Use sequential port numbers 8100-8200 (100 parallel streams)

**4. Transfer Script Design**

**On source (16.26.178.134):**
```bash
#!/bin/bash
# transfer-sender.sh
PORT=$1
FILELIST=$2
cd /dev3
# Create tar archive from file list, compress with pigz, send via netcat
tar cf - -T "$FILELIST" --ignore-failed-read 2>/tmp/sender_${PORT}.log | pigz -1 -p 1 | nc -l -p "$PORT" -q 3
echo "Port $PORT: Transfer complete" >> /tmp/transfer.log
```
Note: `-1` for pigz = fast compression (lower CPU, still good ratio for network transfer)

**On destination (current server):**
```bash
#!/bin/bash
# transfer-receiver.sh
PORT=$1
mkdir -p /dev3
# Receive via netcat, decompress, extract tar (preserves directory structure)
nc -w 180 16.26.178.134 "$PORT" | pigz -d | tar xf - -C /dev3 --keep-old-files 2>/tmp/receiver_${PORT}.log
echo "Port $PORT: Receive complete" >> /tmp/transfer.log
```
Note: `--keep-old-files` ensures no overwrite of existing files (conflict resolution)

**5. Pre-Transfer Preparation**

a) Split missing file list into 100 chunks:
```bash
split -n l/100 /tmp/missing_files.txt /tmp/chunk_
```

b) Copy chunks to source server:
```bash
scp -i /tmp/trn2_key.pem /tmp/chunk_* ubuntu@16.26.178.134:/tmp/
```

c) Update AWS security group to allow inbound TCP on ports 8100-8200:
```bash
# Get security group ID for the instance
SG_ID=$(aws ec2 describe-instances --region ap-southeast-4 --instance-ids i-06469ea258a6b0e1e --query 'Reservations[0].Instances[0].SecurityGroups[0].GroupId' --output text)

# Add inbound rule for ports 8100-8200
aws ec2 authorize-security-group-ingress --region ap-southeast-4 --group-id "$SG_ID" --protocol tcp --port 8100-8200 --cidr 0.0.0.0/0
```

d) Copy transfer scripts to source:
```bash
scp -i /tmp/trn2_key.pem /tmp/transfer-sender.sh ubuntu@16.26.178.134:/tmp/
ssh -i /tmp/trn2_key.pem ubuntu@16.26.178.134 "chmod +x /tmp/transfer-sender.sh"
```

**6. Execute Parallel Transfer**

Launch all 100 streams (ports 8100-8200):

```bash
# Create local receiver script
cat > /tmp/transfer-receiver.sh << 'EOF'
#!/bin/bash
PORT=$1
mkdir -p /dev3
nc -w 180 16.26.178.134 "$PORT" | pigz -d | tar xf - -C /dev3 --keep-old-files 2>/tmp/receiver_${PORT}.log
echo "Port $PORT: Receive complete at $(date)" >> /tmp/transfer.log
EOF
chmod +x /tmp/transfer-receiver.sh

# Launch all receivers in parallel (local)
seq 8100 8200 | parallel -j 100 '/tmp/transfer-receiver.sh {}' &

# Wait 5 seconds for receivers to start listening
sleep 5

# Launch all senders in parallel (remote)
seq 8100 8200 | parallel -j 100 'ssh -i /tmp/trn2_key.pem ubuntu@16.26.178.134 "/tmp/transfer-sender.sh {} /tmp/chunk_{#}"'
```

Note: `{#}` in parallel expands to job number (1-100), matching chunk file indices

**7. Monitor Progress**

Real-time monitoring:
```bash
# Watch active connections
watch -n 2 'ss -tn | grep -E "8[12][0-9]{2}" | wc -l'

# Monitor transfer log
tail -f /tmp/transfer.log

# Watch network throughput
ifstat -i enp71s0 1

# Check for errors in any stream
grep -i error /tmp/receiver_*.log /tmp/sender_*.log
```

**8. Post-Transfer Verification**

a) Check completion status:
```bash
# Count completed streams
grep "complete" /tmp/transfer.log | wc -l
# Should be 200 (100 senders + 100 receivers)

# Identify failed streams
for i in {8100..8200}; do
  if ! grep -q "Port $i" /tmp/transfer.log; then
    echo "Port $i: FAILED"
  fi
done
```

b) Verify file counts and sizes:
```bash
# On destination: count newly transferred files
cd /dev3 && find . -type f -newer /tmp/transfer_start_marker | wc -l

# Compare with expected count
echo "Expected: $(wc -l < /tmp/missing_files.txt)"
```

c) Handle failed streams (if any):
```bash
# Identify which chunks failed
FAILED_PORTS=$(seq 8100 8200 | while read port; do
  grep -q "Port $port" /tmp/transfer.log || echo $port
done)

# Retry failed streams one-by-one
for port in $FAILED_PORTS; do
  chunk_num=$((port - 8100 + 1))
  echo "Retrying port $port (chunk $chunk_num)..."
  /tmp/transfer-receiver.sh $port &
  sleep 2
  ssh -i /tmp/trn2_key.pem ubuntu@16.26.178.134 "/tmp/transfer-sender.sh $port /tmp/chunk_$(printf '%02d' $chunk_num)"
done
```

d) Final verification:
```bash
# Generate new file list on destination
cd /dev3 && find . -type f | sort > /tmp/dest_files_after.txt

# Verify all missing files are now present
comm -23 /tmp/missing_files.txt /tmp/dest_files_after.txt > /tmp/still_missing.txt
echo "Files still missing: $(wc -l < /tmp/still_missing.txt)"
```

content.text: Good! The ports are already open. Now let's create the transfer scripts:

input.content:
#!/bin/bash
PORT=$1
FILELIST=$2
cd /dev3
# Create tar archive from file list, compress with pigz, send via netcat
tar cf - -T "$FILELIST" --ignore-failed-read 2>/tmp/sender_${PORT}.log | pigz -1 -p 1 | nc -l -p "$PORT" -q 3
echo "Port $PORT: Transfer complete at $(date)" >> /tmp/transfer.log

content.text: Good! Connectivity works. The issue is that the receivers started BEFORE the senders, so they timed out waiting. I need to reverse the order - start senders first (which will listen), then start receivers (which will connect). Let me fix the logic:

input.content:
#!/bin/bash
# Generate port-to-chunk mapping
CHUNKS=(aa ab ac ad ae af ag ah ai aj ak al am an ao ap aq ar as at au av aw ax ay az ba bb bc bd be bf bg bh bi bj bk bl bm bn bo bp bq br bs bt bu bv bw bx by bz ca cb cc cd ce cf cg ch ci cj ck cl cm cn co cp cq cr cs ct cu cv)

echo "Step 1: Starting 100 SENDERS on source (they will LISTEN)..."
for i in {0..99}; do
  PORT=$((8100 + i))
  CHUNK="${CHUNKS[$i]}"
  ssh -i /tmp/trn2_key.pem -o StrictHostKeyChecking=no ubuntu@16.26.178.134 "/tmp/transfer-sender.sh $PORT /tmp/chunk_$CHUNK" &
done

# Wait for senders to start listening
echo "Step 2: Waiting 15 seconds for senders to start listening..."
sleep 15

# Launch receivers which will CONNECT to the listening senders
echo "Step 3: Starting 100 RECEIVERS locally (they will CONNECT)..."
seq 8100 8200 | parallel -j 100 '/tmp/transfer-receiver.sh {}'

echo "Step 4: All receivers complete. Waiting for senders to finish..."
wait

echo "Transfer complete! Check /tmp/transfer.log"
